dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit M90 for a cardiac arrest at --Address--.
Hi, I'm Ria, an EMT with Rescue Squad. What made you call 911 tonight?
He was found in the shower unconscious and not breathing, surrounded by needles and drug paraphernalia about 45 minutes ago.
Patient is unresponsive. No eye opening, verbal response or motor response detected. GCS is 3.
No eye movement observed. Patient did not respond to verbal commands or physical stimulation.
No verbal response to loud voice or physical prompt.
No motor response to pain or verbal command. Except for chest compressions initiated by a witness, there's no limb or body movement.
Airway is unobstructed. No breathing. We are placing a King LT airway now with BVM and O2 at 15 liters/min.
No chest movement or breath sounds bilaterally. Skin is pale and dry. SpO2 is 0% per pulse oximetry.
No pulse detected on radial, carotid, or femoral sites. Blood pressure unobtainable. Skin is warm and dry.
Respirations are at 0-no audible or visible chest movement after initial airway placement.
No pulse detected on all three vascular sites after two separate checks by me and partner.
Blood pressure is 0/0. No readings with manual or electronic devices during assessment.
SpO2 is 0%, consistent with lack of oxygen and circulation as seen in the patient's chest area.
Blood glucose measured at 98 mg/dL using a finger-stick test before any procedures.
EKG monitor shows asystole on 4-lead after connecting the electrodes.
Begin manual CPR at 100-120 compressions per minute on left side as per father's consent.
Transitioning to AutoPulse chest compression device to ensure consistent compressions post-manual CPR.
Starting intraosseous access in the left humeral head. Let's go ahead and prepare the site.
Inserting intraosseous access in the left humeral head and securing with a saline lock using aseptic techniques.
Administering first dose of Epi 1:10,000 at 1mg intraosseously as per ACLS-standing orders.
Given 2mg of Naloxone IO as patient was found with drug paraphernalia, opiate overdose suspected.
Placed King I-gel airway. Confirmation of correct insertion with no gastric inflation via inline capnography.
Attaching 4-lead ECG monitoring for continuous cardiac rhythm assessment using standard placement, per ePCR.
Intraosseous access successfully completed in the left humeral head for rapid medication delivery and fluid resuscitation.
Administered 50mEq Sodium Bicarbonate IO as per advanced ACLS protocol to address acidosis or refractory cardiac arrest.
No pain observed. Patient is unresponsive and showed no signs of discomfort during assessment.
Can you tell me more about what happened in the bathroom from when you last saw him until you found him?
I saw him 45 minutes ago. Found him in the shower unconscious, shower running, surrounded by needles and substance abuse paraphernalia.
You mentioned surrounding paraphernalia.
Yes, it looked like hypodermic needles, empty vials-in the bathroom behind the patient.
Skin is pale, dry, and warm. No petechiae or rashes noted on all surfaces from head to toe.
Per ePCR, initiating protocol 2-7: cardiac arrest with initial findings. Contacting medical control soon after.
Checked EKG lead 4 repeatedly in cycles-rhythm remains asystole with no improvement through four rounds of CPR/medications.
Administering second dose of Epinephrine 1:10,000 IO after the second round of chest compressions and interventions.
EKG readings still display asystole with no change in rhythm during the last 4 rounds of care.
Secured patient for transport in standard EMS stretcher, appropriate for monitoring and procedures in transit.
Per medical control guidance, patient will be transported to VCU Emergency Department for on-site confirmation and documentation.
Contacted medical control at VCU regarding prolonged asystole and no ROSC for final determination.
Orders received on 07/09/2020 at 23:40:25 by Dr. Michael Joyce of VCU-cease all resuscitation efforts immediately.
No changes in asystolic rhythm or ROSC observed. Resumed appropriate protocol termination steps.
Double-checking placement of I-gel airway and EKG leads after confirming all drugs administered as per protocol notes.
Documenting final ePCR, including drug dosages, times, EKG tracing notes, and VCU ED orders in preparation for hospital handoff.
Patient remains motionless and non-responsive. All transport team actions are in compliance with termination orders prior to arrival.
All care terminated at 23:40:25. Final vitals and medications known, confirmed no resuscitation to be continued in hospital.
